script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Tuesday — here is your QQQ recap. The portfolio closed two point nine nine percent up on the session.

Micron Technology surged twelve point one seven percent on Tuesday. Sandisk climbed fourteen point two seven. The semiconductor complex didn't just recover from last week's selloff — it led the entire market higher.

The Nasdaq Composite closed higher on Tuesday as chip stocks drove a broad-based rally across major indexes. Semiconductor names outperformed decisively, with investors rotating back into AI-linked hardware ahead of a heavy earnings week. No major macro data prints were on the calendar, leaving market direction entirely in the hands of positioning and momentum.

The top story of Tuesday's session belongs to the memory complex. Micron Technology posted one of the strongest single-session moves in the portfolio, up twelve point one seven percent, and it did not move alone. The setup is straightforward: institutional money is pricing in an accelerating AI infrastructure buildout, and memory is becoming a critical bottleneck. Demand for high-bandwidth memory used in AI training systems has tightened supply materially. Analysts have been raising price targets across the DRAM and NAND space, and Tuesday's move suggests the market is finally catching up to those revisions. With Micron carrying a five point seven five percent portfolio weight, that twelve-plus percent gain was one of the single largest contributors to the portfolio's overall two point nine nine percent session. The stock is now pressing against levels not seen since earlier this year, and the earnings calendar will be the next hard test for whether this momentum holds.

Now the full holdings rundown, largest to smallest.

Nvidia was up one point nine seven percent on Tuesday. The session's semiconductor rally provided a clear tailwind, and Nvidia continues to trade near its fifty-two week high, reflecting sustained institutional conviction in AI infrastructure spending. Watch the earnings cycle closely — peer results this week will set the tone.

Apple was up zero point three five percent on Tuesday. The company raised prices on Apple Music by one dollar per month across individual and student plans, with family and Premier tiers on Apple One also moving higher. The company cited rising licensing costs. Modest move, modest news — but subscription revenue durability remains a key part of the bull thesis.

Alphabet was down one point three eight percent on Tuesday. The stock slipped into earnings, which is a pattern worth noting — the market is demanding proof, not promise. Analysts are watching the three hundred seventy dollar resistance level and flagging a potential inverted head-and-shoulders technical setup. Quarterly results drop Wednesday, and Berkshire Hathaway's new leadership has reportedly accumulated roughly ten billion dollars in Alphabet exposure, signaling long-term confidence from a historically cautious institution.

Micron Technology was up twelve point one seven percent on Tuesday, covered in full in the top story.

Microsoft was down one point one three percent on Tuesday. The stock gave back modest ground ahead of its own earnings window. Analysts have noted the stock looks expensive on the surface but argue patient investors are effectively buying future growth at a discount to intrinsic value. Near-term, execution on Azure AI adoption is the number to watch.

Amazon was down zero point nine eight percent on Tuesday. The pullback was contained and largely reflects broader caution around consumer discretionary exposure heading into earnings. AWS growth and advertising revenue are the two metrics that will matter most when results land.

Advanced Micro Devices was up eight point one one percent on Tuesday. The stock rode the semiconductor rally hard, benefiting directly from the same AI infrastructure narrative lifting Micron. No company-specific news drove the move — this was sector momentum, and it worked decisively in this portfolio's favor.

Tesla was up two point five three percent on Tuesday. Earnings are due Wednesday, making Tuesday's gain a straightforward pre-report positioning move. Investors are watching for any update on autonomous vehicle timelines and energy storage volumes alongside the core automotive numbers.

Intel was up eight point six four percent on Tuesday. That is a significant single-session move for a stock that has faced sustained fundamental pressure. No specific catalyst emerged, but the broad semiconductor recovery lifted Intel alongside its peers. This is a name where momentum and fundamentals remain disconnected — watch for any foundry business updates.

Turning to one secondary holding worth flagging: Applied Materials was up seven point three nine percent on Tuesday. As a key supplier of semiconductor manufacturing equipment, the company moved in direct sympathy with the memory rally. When Micron and Sandisk surge, the equipment layer tends to follow.

Three things to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, Alphabet reports Wednesday — the quarter will set the tone for the entire communication services and AI advertising complex. Second, Tesla reports Wednesday alongside Alphabet — autonomous driving commentary and margin trends will drive the reaction. Third, watch for any Federal Reserve speaker appearances later in the week, as rate trajectory commentary will directly affect growth equity valuations across this entire portfolio.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Wednesday's Alphabet and Tesla earnings reports are the central event — the numbers will either validate this week's rally or put it under immediate pressure.
